title: Best Anchorages Between Estepona and Cabopino
subtitle: A skipper's guide to nine sheltered anchorages along the 28 NM of coast between Estepona and Cabopino, with depths, holding notes and wind windows for planning a half-day or full-day charter from Puerto Banus.
author: BoatHire24 Guides

sections.heading: The strip at a glance
sections.html:
The 28 nautical miles between Estepona marina and Cabopino hold roughly a dozen workable anchorages, and on a good summer day you can string three of them into a single charter. This guide is the skipper-side view: where the sand actually lies, which bays die first in a Levante, and how long the runs really take from Puerto Banus. All distances are measured from the Banus harbour mouth, and every depth, holding note and wind window here comes from boats that work this water week in and week out through the season.

Think of the coast as three zones. West of Puerto Banus, taking in Guadalmina, San Pedro and Estepona, gives you Poniente shelter and quieter water in August. East of Banus, where you find Rio Verde, the Marbella roadstead, Cala del Faro and Cabopino, has the cleaner sand and the dramatic lighthouse-and-dune backdrop. The middle, Banus itself, is the busiest mile of coast in Andalusia between June and September, so we treat it as transit rather than swim water. Once you understand those three zones, the whole strip reads like a map you can plan against rather than a wall of beach.

The single number that decides everything is the wind. A Levante is the easterly that funnels up the Strait of Gibraltar and piles short, steep chop onto the east-facing bays. A Poniente is the westerly that does the reverse. The headlands break the fetch, so the lee side of the coast almost always has a flat, swimmable bay somewhere on it. The skill is reading the morning forecast and matching it to the right zone, which is exactly what your skipper does at the dock before you cast off.

AnchorageFrom Banus (NM)Depth (m)Best wind
Estepona Bay10 W5-8Levante
Guadalmina4 W4-6Light any
Rio Verde1.5 E4-6Light Poniente
Levante Beach0.5 E4-5Light Poniente
Marbella roadstead4 E5-7Poniente
Cala del Faro2 E5-7Poniente
Cabopino / Cala Cortes8 E4-8Poniente

sections.heading: Cala del Faro, the safe default
sections.html:
Two nautical miles east of Banus, tucked under the Marbella lighthouse cliffs, this is the bay we go to nine times out of ten on a Tier A charter. The bottom is sand in 5-7 m, holding is excellent on a Danforth, and the cliff to the north breaks any breeze under about 18 kts of Poniente. Water clears up by 0900 most mornings and you can usually see your anchor on the bottom. It is also the closest swimmable anchorage to Banus, which matters a great deal on a 2-hour booking where every minute of transit is a minute lost from the water.

What makes Cala del Faro the reliable choice is not just the shelter, it is the forgiving combination of clean sand and a short run home. On a hot July afternoon you can drop the hook here, give everyone an hour in the water, and still be back at the pontoon comfortably inside your slot. The lighthouse and the cliffs behind give the swim stop a proper sense of place rather than the generic feel of anchoring off a flat beach, and the depth band keeps you well clear of the shore break and the swim-zone buoy lines. For a first charter, or for anyone who simply wants a sure thing, this is the bay we recommend without hesitation.
sections.image_caption: The Astondoa 40, a Tier A boat and the usual choice for a Cala del Faro swim stop.

sections.heading: Cabopino and Cala Cortes, the clearest water
sections.html:
Eight NM east, past the Marbella sewage outfall and the Rio Real, you cross a clean line: visibility jumps to 8-12 m and the seabed turns to white sand backed by the protected Artola dunes. Anchor in 4-8 m about 300 m off the beach, outside the swim-zone buoys. Cabopino marina is two minutes away if you want a long lunch ashore, which makes this the one anchorage on the strip where you can combine the cleanest water with a proper meal off the boat. It is 8 NM east of Banus, about 25 minutes at 20 kts cruise, so it eats roughly an hour of your day round-trip.

That hour is the reason Cabopino is a full-day proposition rather than a quick stop. On the 8-hour day at €2,299 there is plenty of slack to absorb the run, and the reward is the best snorkelling and the most photogenic backdrop on the whole coast. The Artola dunes are a protected natural monument, so the shoreline behind the anchorage stays undeveloped and wild in a way that is increasingly rare on this stretch. Families tend to love it because the kids get a genuine sandbar to swim over, and groups celebrating something like it because the marina lunch turns a charter into a proper day out. This is the obvious destination for a full-day charter on the Astondoa 40 or Azimut 39.
sections.image_caption: The Azimut 39 from above, the kind of flybridge motor yacht that makes the Cabopino run an easy full day.

sections.heading: Quick stops east of Banus
sections.html:
If you have 90 minutes of swim time and the wind is light, the bays immediately east of Banus work fine. Rio Verde at 1.5 NM holds in 4-6 m on sand with patches of weed, and your skipper picks the patch rather than trusting the chart. Levante Beach, just outside the breakwater at half a nautical mile, is the closest possible stop but gets busy with charter tenders and jet skis from noon onwards. We use it mainly as a calm-weather warm-up before pushing east to cleaner water.

These two are the workhorses of the short charter. On a two-hour booking where you cannot justify the run to Cabopino, dropping into Rio Verde or Levante Beach buys you swim time you would otherwise spend at cruise speed. The trade is water quality: both sit close to the busiest mile of coast in the region, so on a peak August afternoon the surface traffic is real and the visibility is honest rather than spectacular. In light Poniente before the midday crowd builds, though, they do exactly what you need, and the skipper will always nudge you a little further along the coast if conditions allow it.

sections.heading: The Estepona side, your Levante backup
sections.html:
When the easterly fills in above 15 kts, the eastern bays turn into a short, ugly chop on a lee shore and we run west. The roadstead immediately east of Estepona marina sits under Punta de la Plata and stays workable up to about 20 kts of Levante, because the headland breaks the fetch before it reaches you. Holding is sand in 5-8 m. The run from Banus is 10 NM, which is about 28 minutes at 22 kts on a Pershing 46 or a Mangusta 80, and slightly longer on the Tier A boats that cruise nearer 18 kts.

The Estepona side is worth knowing about even when the weather is fine, because it is genuinely quieter than the Banus end in the peak weeks. West of San Pedro the coast opens out, the beaches get longer and emptier, and the run down past Guadalmina has a calm, residential character that suits a slow morning on the water. Guadalmina itself, at 4 NM west, holds in 4-6 m and works in light air from almost any direction, which makes it a useful first stop when the forecast is settled and you simply want to ease into the day before the wind builds at 1500. Treat the whole western zone as your insurance: when the Levante kills the east, this is where the swimmable water has gone.

sections.heading: Anchorages we skip, and reading the day
sections.html:
Local knowledge is as much about where not to drop the hook as where to. The Guadalmina and Rio Verde mouths after heavy rain drop visibility to nothing for a day or two, because the runoff carries silt straight out over the anchorage. The stretch directly off San Pedro has more weed than the chart suggests and worse holding than it looks, so an anchor that bites first time elsewhere can drag here. The kilometre of water east of Marbella marina sits over the municipal outfall, which is fine to transit but not somewhere we drop the hook for a swim. None of this shows up on a basic chart plot, which is exactly why a licensed local skipper matters more than the app on your phone.

A chart will tell you the depth and the bottom type in broad strokes, but it will not tell you that the weed off San Pedro has crept beyond where the survey marks it, or that a river mouth two miles upwind has just dumped a day of rain into the bay. That margin between what the chart says and what the water actually does is the whole value of having someone aboard who runs this coast every week through the summer.

The forecast call itself gets made at the dock, not the night before. We look at three numbers: wind direction, wind strength at 1500 which is the daily peak, and swell period. Anything under 12 kts and you can pick anywhere on the strip, so the choice becomes about water quality and what you want from the day rather than shelter. At 12-18 kts we pick the lee side, east bays for Poniente and west bays for Levante, and the day plans itself around that single decision. Above 18 kts we either tuck deep into Cala del Faro or Estepona Bay, where the cliffs and headlands give the most shelter, or we shorten the day. We do not push weather windows on charter, because it is your day off and not a delivery run.

The 1500 peak matters more than most guests expect. A coast that is glassy at 1000 can build a real chop by mid-afternoon as the land heats and the sea breeze fills in, so an early start often buys you the calmest water of the day. If you have any flexibility on departure time, taking the morning slot is the single best thing you can do for the quality of your swim stops, and it is the one piece of advice we give almost every guest who asks how to make the most of a charter here.

sections.heading: How many stops fit in a day
sections.html:
On a 2-hour Tier A charter at €749 all-in you get one stop, almost always Cala del Faro, because the maths of transit time leaves room for nothing else. On 4 hours you get two stops, typically Rio Verde plus Cala del Faro, or Cala del Faro plus a quick Cabopino tease if the water is calm. On the full 8-hour day at €2,299 you can string Estepona Bay or Guadalmina in the morning, Cala del Faro for lunch on board, and Cabopino in the afternoon, which is about as complete a tour of the strip as the coast allows.

Plan transits at 18-22 kts and budget 45 minutes per swim stop minimum, otherwise the day starts to feel rushed and you spend it watching the clock instead of the water. The Mangusta 80 at €4,719 for 4 hours, with a jet ski included, covers the same ground roughly 25% faster than the Tier A boats and lets you skip further down towards Sotogrande if you want to, which is what makes the bigger boat worth it on a half-day rather than just more deck space. Bigger or different boats, such as the Pershing 46, Ferretti 94 and Canados 86, are priced on request. The right answer is usually less about the boat and more about how much time you are prepared to spend at cruise versus at anchor.
sections.image_caption: The Mangusta 80 at cruise, the boat that covers the Estepona-to-Cabopino strip roughly 25% faster on a half-day.

sections.heading: The fleet behind these anchorages
sections.html:
The boats decide how the coast plays. The Astondoa 40, a 12.5 m motor yacht built by the long-established Spanish yard Astondoa, carries up to 9 guests across two private cabins and is the boat we put on most Cala del Faro days. The Azimut 39 is an Italian flybridge motor yacht, also 12.5 m, that takes up to 11 guests with two cabins plus a convertible saloon and cruises around 18 knots, which is what makes the full Estepona-to-Cabopino run comfortable inside an 8-hour day. Both are Tier A, both include a licensed Spanish skipper, and both launch from Puerto Banus.

At the top of the range the Mangusta 80 is a 24 m open sport yacht from Overmarine, the Italian builder whose Mangusta line has been a fixture of the fast luxury motor-yacht world for decades. It carries up to 12 guests, comes with a captain and deckhand, and at €4,719 for a 4-hour minimum charter it includes a jet ski. The extra speed and waterline length are not just about comfort; they genuinely change which anchorages are reachable in a given window, opening up the longer runs west towards Sotogrande or letting a half-day take in stops that would be a stretch on a slower boat.

sections.heading: What to bring and how the day runs
sections.html:
Charters here are deliberately easy to pack for, because the boat carries most of what you need. We supply towels, snorkel masks and a paddle board on most boats, so you do not have to drag your own gear down to the pontoon. What is genuinely worth bringing yourself is high-factor sun cream, a hat, a light layer for the run home when the apparent wind picks up, and soft-soled shoes or none at all to keep the decks clean. Leave hard cases and heels behind; everything aboard runs better barefoot.

The rhythm of a typical day is simple. You board at Puerto Banus, the skipper runs the safety brief and reads the forecast with you, and then you head for whichever zone the wind has chosen. There is a transit out at cruise, the anchor goes down on sand with the skipper setting it under reverse thrust, and then the time is yours to swim, snorkel, paddle board or simply sit on the bathing platform with a drink. On a longer charter you repeat that two or three times, with lunch either aboard or ashore at Cabopino, before an easy run back into the marina. The skipper stays aboard the whole time you are in the water, so there is always someone watching the boat and the swimmers.

sections.heading: Seasonality and the best time to go
sections.html:
The Costa del Sol gives you a long charter season, but the water itself has a clear sweet spot. Summer water temperature runs 22-25 °C from late June to mid-September, which is when the swim stops are at their best and the sea feels genuinely warm rather than bracing. Visibility on calm mornings sits at 6-12 m across the strip, with the eastern bays around Cabopino at the clear end of that range and the busier Banus water at the murkier end. Those are the numbers that should shape when you book and which anchorages you prioritise.

July and August are the warmest and busiest, with the most surface traffic and the strongest case for an early start to beat both the crowds and the afternoon sea breeze. June and the first half of September are arguably the better windows for a charter that is mostly about the anchorages: the water is still warm, the visibility is just as good on a calm day, and the coast is noticeably quieter. Whenever you come, the wind rules apply the same way, so the planning logic in this guide holds across the season. The only real variable is how much company you will have in the popular bays.

sections.heading: What is included, and how to book
sections.html:
Every anchor stop comes with the same all-inclusive package, with no hidden bar bill at the end:

  • Licensed Spanish skipper who actually knows these bays
  • Fuel for the whole charter, with no surcharge
  • Water, soft drinks, beer, white wine and cava on ice
  • Light snacks aboard
  • Towels, snorkel masks and a paddle board on most boats
  • Full insurance and safety equipment, Spanish IVA at 21%

The drinks list is honest and unlimited within reason, and the price you book is the price you pay. On the water, the skipper sets the hook with reverse thrust and stays aboard the entire time you swim, and we never anchor inside the swim-zone buoy lines, which run roughly 200 m off most beaches. Booking is simple: pick a date, tell us your group size and what you want from the day, and we will match you to the right boat and the right zone. The 2-hour Tier A slot at €749 is the easiest way to try the coast; the 8-hour day at €2,299 is the one that lets you see all of it from Estepona to Cabopino in a single charter.

callout.html:
